.
unmanned motorcycles and bicycles, have gained increas-
0 S
ing attention from researchers due to their advantages of high
1
4 speed, narrow body and exceptional maneuverability compared
2 to multi-track robots, multi-wheel robots, and legged robots

[1]–[6]. However,  the stationary instability of STTW robots
v
i
hinders them from performing complex tasks, such as those
X requiring  intermittent  stops,  thus  impeding  their  practical
r
a application [7], [8].
The challenges  faced by stationary balance control  can
be categorized into two dimensions. Firstly,  the balancing
mechanism at high speeds becomes ineffective at low speeds,
resulting  in  the  requirement  of  additional  actuators  (e.g.
pendulum,  reaction wheel,  control moment gyro)  [9]–[12].
Secondly, STTW robots are affected by both matched and
mismatched disturbances [13], [14], and existing disturbance
rejection methods cannot effectively address these disturbances
This work was supported in part by National Natural Science Foundation of

Beijing  100084,  China  (email:  by-wang19@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn;
dengyang@mail.tsinghua.edu.cn;  jfl21@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn;
cz da@tsinghua.edu.cn; bliang@tsinghua.edu.cn.)
Yiyong Sun is with the School of Aerospace Engineering, Beijing Institute
of Technology, Beijing 100081, China. (email: sunyy@bit.edu.cn)

ased Control for Track
Two-Wheeled Robots
ing, Yiyong Sun, Member, IEEE,
in Liang, Senior Member, IEEE
in nonlinear, non-minimum phase systems. Therefore,  it  is
necessary to address two key problems: (i) Is there any elegant
physical mechanism for stationary balance? (ii) How can the
robot  handle matched and mismatched disturbances while
ensuring high tracking accuracy?
A. Mechanisms for Stationary Balance
Balance control of STTW robots is mainly based on steering
motion, where the fundamental principle involves controlling
the centrifugal force generated by steering to counteract  the
destabilizing effect of gravity [8], [15]. However, a low speed
cannot generate sufficient centrifugal  force, making balance
control unattainable. To address this issue, previous studies
have examined a variety of strategies,  including mainly four
techniques: pendulum [9],  reaction wheel  [10], control mo-
ment gyroscope (CMG) [11], [12], and steering control with
negative  trail  [7],  [8]. However,  the CMG and pendulum
schemes introduce additional mass and actuators, while the
reaction wheel  scheme  is not  suitable for  larger vehicles,
and the negative trail  scheme has a significantly restricted
attraction domain [7]. As a result, none of these techniques
achieves a graceful stationary balance.
We have observed a unique stationary balance maneuver
performed by human cyclists, known as the track stand [16],
[17]. This maneuver involves maintaining the handlebar at a
specific angle and achieving balance through slight forward
and backward movements of  the rear wheel,  as shown in
Fig. 1. Existing research has not well studied the track stand
maneuver except for a series of studies by Huang et al. [18],
[19], which, however, only investigated the track stand maneu-
ver of an uncommon structure with a motor-driven front wheel.
The long-term disregard of this intriguing maneuver is partially
due to the difficulty of modeling STTW robots. First, previous
studies  typically employed the Lagrangian method [2]–[8],
resulting in a dynamic model lacking intuitive interpretation.
Moreover, many studies directly controlled the velocity of
the rear wheel  [8],  [14],  [20],  rather  than the torque,  thus
overlooking the effect of the rear wheel on balance control. To
gain a more intuitive understanding of the principles behind
track stand maneuver, we adopt the Newton-Euler method to
establish the dynamics of a typical STTW robot equipped with
a steering motor and a rear wheel motor. Through the equation
of motion, we provide an interpretation for the mechanism of
the track stand maneuver.

Fig. 1.  (a)/(b) The STTW robot is performing track stand on a laterally/longit
rear position reference.
B.

Methods for Matched/Mismatched Disturbance Rejection
For  the stationary balance  task, STTW robots  face  the
challenge of matched and mismatched disturbances [1], [13].
Our goal is to design a disturbance rejection scheme to achieve
accurate tracking and disturbance rejection simultaneously.
Compared  to  robust  control methods  such as H2/H∞
control and sliding mode control (SMC), disturbance observer-
based control (DOBC) has the advantages of flexibility and
low conservatism [21], which has gained widespread appli-
cations, such as robotics [22],  [23], motor drives [24]–[26],
hypersonic vehicles [27], mechatronic systems [28], [29], and
power  systems  [30]. The core  idea of DOBC is using a
disturbance observer (DO) to estimate disturbances and then
introduce an equal and opposite quantity in the control input
to counteract  them,  that  is, u = α(x) − dˆ, where, α(x)
denotes a nominal feedback control law and dˆ represents an
estimated disturbance. However, this approach is only effective
for matched disturbances.
For mismatched disturbances, Chen et al.  [21] proposed
nonlinear disturbance observer-based robust control  (NDO-
BRC) for nonlinear single-input single-output (SISO) systems
with mismatched disturbances, which introduced a disturbance
compensation gain vector  into the control  law,  in the form
u = α(x) + β(x)dˆ,  to eliminate mismatched disturbances
from the output channel. However, the calculation of the gain
β(x)  is complex and is coupled with the nominal control
law α(x). Yang et al.  [24] developed a generalized DOBC
method for multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) nonlinear
systems, where the systems are assumed to be capable of
state feedback linearization. Meanwhile,  in various control
fields, researchers have developed method-specific techniques
for mismatched disturbance attenuation. For linear feedback
control, Li et al. [28] proposed the generalized extended state
observer-based control  (GESOBC)  for  linear SISO/MIMO
systems with mismatched disturbances, the mind behind which
is similar to [21], and the disturbance compensation gain also
depends on the nominal control gain. Regarding backstepping
control, Sun et al.  [27],  [30],  [31] combined the nonlinear
disturbance observer with back-stepping technique to achieve
mismatched disturbance attenuation, where estimated distur-
bances are  integrated into the design of  the backstepping
controller in each step. As for SMC, Yang et al. [25], [29] and

2
udinally inclined plane with varying angles. (c) The STTW robot is tracking a
Ginoya et al. [32], [33] have developed a series of disturbance
observer-based SMCs, which achieve mismatched disturbance
attenuation by designing a modified nonlinear sliding mode
surface considering the estimated disturbance. However,  the
systems  in SMC are  limited to chains of  integrators,  and
thus these methods are not suitable for non-minimum phase
systems.
These works have separately addressed mismatched distur-
bance attenuation, but have not offered a unified solution. Our
previous work [13] proposed a universal disturbance rejec-
tion scheme, equilibrium compensation-based control (ECBC),
which is suitable for matched/mismatched disturbances and
general  linear systems,  including non-minimum phase sys-
tems.  In this article, we extend this method to nonlinear
systems, resulting in a general disturbance rejection scheme
named equilibrium adaptation-based control (EABC).
Our core insight  is  that both the compensation methods
for matched disturbances and those sophisticated methods for
mismatched disturbances can be interpreted uniformly as the
adaptation of equilibrium points to disturbances. Specifically,
the EABC scheme involves three key steps: estimating the dis-
turbance through existing disturbance observation techniques,
calculating or optimizing  the disturbed equilibrium points
under the requirement of zero steady-state tracking error, and
finally regulating the state to the disturbed equilibrium point
using standard controllers.
C. Contribution and Outline
In summary, the main contributions of this work include:
1) We provide an interpretation for the mechanism of the
track stand maneuver of STTW robots. To the best of  the
authors’ knowledge, this work is the first study accomplishing
the track stand maneuver with only one steering motor and
one rear motor, without additional actuators.
2) We propose the EABC scheme for mismatched distur-
bance rejection of nonlinear systems, which can be seamlessly
integrated with existing controllers and disturbance observers.
In addition, we provide several conditions that ensure accurate
tracking with bounded error or zero steady-state error.
3) We integrate the EABC with nonlinear MPC for track
stand control of STTW robots. Experimental results demon-
strate that our method significantly reduces tracking errors by
several orders of magnitude..

A. Problem Formulation
Consider a perturbed nonlinear system:
x˙ = f(x) +Gu(x)u +Gd(x)d

tively. f(x),Gu(x),Gd(x), hm(x), ho(x) are smooth func-
tions with appropriate dimensions.
Definition 1 (  [13],  [34],  [35]): A disturbance d in d
is classified as a matched disturbance if  the corresponding
column gd(x)  in Gd(x)  lies within the range of Gu(x).
That  is,  there exists a nonzero vector function λ(x) satis-
fying Gu(x)λ(x) = gd(x). Otherwise,  it  is a mismatched
disturbance.
Assumption 1: The disturbance d is assumed to be a slow-
varying disturbance. Namely, it is differentiable and bounded,
satisfying |d(t)| ⪯ d  , and its derivative d˙  is bounded,

Remark 1: Assumption 1 is more general  than those in
previous studies on mismatched disturbance(e.g.  [13],  [28]),
where the disturbance converges to a constant value as time
goes to infinity. Representative disturbances include: constant
parameter perturbation (e.g. additional unknown payload), sen-
sor bias (e.g. inertial measurement unit equipment offset), and
slow-varying external disturbances (e.g. slow-varying ground
inclination angle).
Problem statement: For  system (1) with slow-varying
disturbances that may be matched or mismatched, the objective
is to design a disturbance rejection framework that achieves
accurate tracking without steady-state error.
Solution: The EABC scheme is shown in Fig. 2, which
is based on the idea that tracking accuracy only depends on
the equilibrium point  that  the system converges  to. Under
slow-varying disturbances, the system first estimates the dis-
turbances by general disturbance observers, then calculates an

3
Fig. 2.  The general EABC disturbance rejection scheme.
appropriate disturbed equilibrium trajectory ensuring tracking
performance, and finally drives the state to it using a general
regulation controller.

So far, all the components of EABC have been presented.
To introduce the properties of EABC, we define the nominal

C. Properties of EABC
Theorem 1: Consider the system (1) controlled by the EABC
(3)–(5)–(7). If Assumption 1 holds and the EABC satisfies the
following conditions:
1) The observation error system (4) is input-to-state stable;
2) The equilibrium equation (5) has more than one solution;
3) The nominal  regulation error system (10)  is input-to-
state stable;
4) Function κ(x˜) is Lipschitz continuous;
5) Function Gu(x) and Gd(x) are bounded, denoted as
|Gu(x)| ⪯ Gu,b, |Gd(x)| ⪯ Gd,b;
then the following conclusions hold:
a) The controlled output yo tracks the reference yref with
bounded error.
b) Moreover,  if the disturbance derivative d˙ converges to
zero,  then the tracking error y˜o will also converge to
zero.
Proof: Since κ(xˆ −xˆe) = κ(x−ex −xˆe) = κ(x˜ −ex),
by substituting uc(xˆ) for u in (1),  the closed-loop system
under EABC (3)–(5)–(7) is given by
x˙ = f(x) +G (x)u (x) +G (x)dˆ+ ϵ,

According to Assumption 1 and Condition 1), the estimation
errors ex, ed are bounded, denoted as |ex| ⪯ ex,b, |ed| ⪯ ed,b.
According to Condition 4), for the i-th row of κ, there exists
a positive constant mi > 0 such that
|κi(x˜ − ex) − κi(x˜)| ≤ mi ∥ex∥ ≤ mi ∥ex,b∥ .  (13)
Thus, we have

Gd(x) are bounded, so ϵ is bounded and satisfies
|ϵ| = |Gd(x)ed +Gu(x)(κ(x˜ − ex) − κ(x˜))|

According to Condition 3), the regulation error state x˜ and the
tracking error y˜o are also bounded. Thus Conclusion a) holds.
Furthermore,  if d˙ → 0 as t → ∞, since (4) is input-to-
state stable, we have e¯ → 0. From (15), ϵ → 0. Applying the
property of input-to-state stability of (10), we can deduce that
x˜ → 0, y˜o → 0, which gives Conclusion b).
Remark 4: According to (15), even if the disturbance deriva-
tive d˙ does not converge to zero, we can still decrease the
tracking error by improving the performance of the disturbance
observer, specifically by reducing the estimation error e¯ closer
to zero.
Remark 5: Since (5) is a necessary condition for equilibrium
points and accurate tracking,  if the equilibrium equation (5)
does not have a solution, the system cannot accurately regulate
the controlled output yo to the reference yref .
D. Relation with Existing Methods
To the best knowledge of the authors, although the mind
behind EABC is intuitive, it has not been reported in existing
literature, except for our prior study of ECBC [13], [14], which
is the linear case of EABC. EABC draws inspiration from
nonlinear disturbance observer-based control (NDOBC) [21],
[23], [35], [40] but provides a novel point of view.
1) Relation with ECBC: EABC and ECBC [13], [14] are
based on the same process: first estimating the disturbance by
disturbance observers, then solving the disturbed equilibrium
point and finally regulating the state to that equilibrium point
by general controllers. However, ECBC is only suitable for
linear systems, while EABC is applicable to nonlinear systems.
2) Relation with NDOBC: From the original concept of
NDOBC [23], EABC can be considered a special case of
NDOBC. From the perspective of specific applications, EABC
differs fundamentally in its underlying principles compared to
NDOBCs for matched disturbances [23], [40] and mismatched
disturbances [21]. These NDOBCs try to design a controller
with respect to the state and the estimated disturbance, with
the formulation:
u  = κ(xˆ) − β(xˆ)dˆ,  (16)
NDOBC.

metadata.section_title: Track Stand Model and EABC Design

text:
In Section III-A, we first establish the dynamics of  the
STTW robot using the Newton-Euler method, by which we can
obtain an intuitive physical interpretation of the mechanism of
track stand maneuver. Then, we detail the EABC design for
track stand control in Section III-B.
A. Track Stand Model
The schematic of the STTW robot developed in this study
is shown in Fig. 3a. The notation used is listed in Table I. We
make the following assumptions during modeling: (i) the front
and rear suspensions are locked; (ii) there is no lateral slippage
between the wheels and the ground; (iii) the thicknesses of the
wheels are negligible. Detailed steps of the modeling process
are provided in the Appendix-A. Here, we focus on presenting
the key steps and results.
According to Fig. 3b, consider the roll motion, the robot is
subject  to gravity Fg,  inertial force Fi, centrifugal force Fc
and normal force N. The roll dynamics is given by

T  T

0, satisfying fsys(xe, ue, 0) = 0, the locally linearized system
is controllable.
Interpretation of  the mechanism behind the track stand:
Through  (18),  the physical  principle governing  the  track
stand maneuver can be stated as follows: The inertial force
generated by the forward and backward movement of  the

Fig. 4.  Block diagram of the control system for the robot.
rear wheel, under  the geometric constraints  introduced by
steering, produces a lateral  force component mao,y, which
is  leveraged  to counteract  the gravitational  force,  thereby
enabling maintaining balance at stand still. Furthermore, with
a natural steering angle, δ ∈ (−π, π), the larger the steering
angle,  the more “balance torque” the track stand maneuver
can provide. If the steering angle is equal to zero, no “balance
torque” can be generated.
B. EABC Design for Track Stand Control
The detailed EABC design for the STTW robot is illustrated
in Fig. 4, comprising an extended Kalman filter for disturbance
observation, a disturbed equilibrium estimator, and a nonlinear
MPC as the nominal regulator.
1) System Specification: For the track stand task, the robot
is expected to track a traveled distance sref and a steering
angle δref . According to dynamics (38),  the terms  in the
perturbed nonlinear system (1) are given by

metadata.section_title: Track Stand Model and EABC Design

text:
We use the second method in our
experiments.
According to Lemma 4.5 in [39], the linear estimation error
system (24) is globally exponentially stable at the origin, and
thus the system is input-to-state stable, satisfying Condition
1) in Theorem 1.
3) Disturbed Equilibrium Estimator: For track stand con-
trol, to estimate disturbed equilibrium, the equilibrium equa-
tion (5) should be solved. According to Definition 1, dr is a
matched disturbance, while dφ is a mismatched disturbance.
ˆ  ˆ  ˆ T
Under the estimated disturbance d = [dr, dφ]  ,  the solution
to the equilibrium equation (5) has the form of
T  ˆ  T
xˆe = [sref , 0, δref , φˆe, 0]  , uˆe = [−dr, 0]  ,  (25)
(cid:32)  ac  ˆ (cid:33)

φˆe = sin  .  (26)
mgh
Condition 5) is satisfied, provided that (25) and (26) are valid.
4) GNMS-Based Nonlinear MPC:  To make use of  the
system’s nonlinear dynamics, we choose nonlinear MPC as
the  regulation controller, which has been validated  to be
effective in robots such as quadruped robots and drones. The
procedure of nonlinear MPC is  iteratively solving a finite-
horizon, receding nonlinear optimal control problem (NOCP),
which can be solved by shooting methods or direct collocation,
where the original NOCP is transcribed into a nonlinear pro-
gramming problem solved by a general solver. However, such
methods are not fast enough for real-time MPC applications.
Recently, GNMS methods  [41]–[43] have been developed
to solve NOCPs by leveraging their sparsity structure using
Riccati recursion, ensuring that their computing speed meets
the requirement of running in real time.
The receding NOCP problem is given by

where x˜ = x−xˆe, u˜ = u−uˆe, Q is a positive semidefinite
matrix and R is a positive definite weighting matrix. In this
context, we assume that the conditions about the cost function
for the input-to-state stability of MPC, as outlined in [44], [45],
are satisfied. The goal of the NOCP is to find the following
feedforward and feedback control law to stabilize the closed-
loop system:
u(t) = uff (t) +K(t)(x − xref ),  (28)
where uff (t)  is  the feedforward control and K(t)  is  the
feedback control gain..

metadata.section_title: Experimental Validation of the EABC Scheme

text:
In this section, we deploy the EABC on an STTW robot test
bench, as depicted in Fig. 5, to validate its disturbance rejec-
tion capability through two experiments. The first scenario is
the inclined plane track stand (Section IV-B), which validates
the capability to reject a varying external disturbance. The
second scenario is the rear position tracking (Section IV-C),
where the robot tracks a varying reference.
A. Experimental Setup
1) Hardware Description: The parameters of  the STTW
robot are listed in Table II. The nonlinear MPC was developed
based on the Control Toolbox library [46] and deployed on a
Raspberry Pi 4B module. The MPC optimization thread (27)
runs at 100 Hz and the feedback controller (28) runs at 200 Hz.
The rear torque command is sent directly to the rear motor,
while the steering velocity command is sent  to the steering
joint, where a low-level proportional-integral  (PI) controller
for velocity tracking runs at 1000 Hz.
2) Comparison Setting:  In the experiments,  to verify the
EABC’s capability of eliminating slow-varying disturbances,
we compare the EABC-MPC presented in this article with
three controllers. The first  is based on the ECBC scheme
proposed in [13] (ECBC-MPC). The second is an integrator-
based MPC [47] (IMPC), where the state is augmented by
incorporating the integral of  the tracking error. The last  is
a  simple MPC controller without  a disturbance  rejection
mechanism (MPC).
B. Track Stand on an Inclined Plane
In this experimental scenario, the robot performs the track
stand maneuver on a 1m× 1m board with a varying inclined

7
angle, as depicted in Fig. 6(b), demonstrating the rejection
capability to a varying external disturbance. Initially, the board
is placed horizontally. Once the robot is stabilized, the board
is tilted longitudinally or laterally, thus applying longitudinal
or  lateral disturbances to the robot. The angle is gradually
increased until the robot loses balance.
Fig. 6(a) shows  the experimental  results under  the four
controllers,  and Fig.  6(c) presents  the comparison of  the
maximum angles and position tracking error of  the robot
during the experiments.  It  should be noted that  since  the
inclination angle of  the board cannot be directly measured,
Fig. 6(c) presents the pitch and roll angles measured by the
robot’s inertial measurement unit (IMU). The pitch angle is
approximately equal  to the longitudinal  inclination angle of
the board, and the roll angle is positively correlated with the
lateral  inclination angle. Thus, such angles still characterize
the robot’s disturbance rejection capabilities under different
controllers. In Fig. 6(c), for the roll/pitch angle, larger values
indicate stronger disturbance rejection capabilities;  for  rear
position errors, smaller values represent higher tracking ac-
curacy. The best values in Fig. 6(c) are highlighted in bold
and surrounded by boxes.
It is clear that, both in the laterally inclined and longitudi-
nally inclined scenarios, both the disturbance rejection capabil-
ity and the tracking accuracy of the EABC-MPC outperform
those under  the ECBC-MPC,  IMPC and simple MPC. The
EABC-MPC controller can maintain the balance of the robot
◦
under a maximum roll disturbance of 2.81  and a maximum
◦
pitch disturbance of 13.31  with a position tracking error
smaller  than 0.05 m.  It  is remarkable that  in the laterally
inclined plane,  the performance of  the EABC method far
exceeds that of the other methods.
C. Rear Position Tracking
In this experiment,  the robot  tracks a reference command
for the rear wheel position while performing the track stand,
as  shown  in Fig.  7(b),  aiming  to showcase  the  tracking
performance of a varying reference. The initial position is
0 m, and then every 16 s, the command switches to 0.15 m,
0 m, −0.15 m, and returns  to 0 m,  in sequence. To avoid
command jumps, a first-order low-pass filter is used to smooth
the command during switches. The experiments are carried out
with the EABC-MPC, ECBC-MPC, IMPC and simple MPC
controllers, five times for each controller. The mean value and
the 1-σ region of the five sets of experimental data are plotted
in Fig. 7(a), represented by solid lines and shaded areas with
light colors,  respectively. Additionally, RMSE (Root Mean
Square Error) and MAE (Mean Absolute Error) are calculated
for position/steering tracking,  for each set of data, and for
every controller. Then the mean and standard deviation of the
RMSEs/MAEs for the five sets of data are calculated. These
statistics are illustrated in Fig. 7(c), where the main bar value
represents the mean of the five RMSEs/MAEs, and the error
bar indicates its standard deviation.
Through Fig. 7(a) and Fig. 7(c),  it can be observed that
the EABC-MPC controller achieves accurate tracking in both
the rear position (RMSE: 0.02 m and MAE: 0.01 m) and the

In this article, we answer two questions related to station-
ary balance control of STTW robots. For  the first question
regarding the stationary balancing mechanism, we propose
utilizing the track stand maneuver and provide an intuitive
physical interpretation of this motion. For the second question
concerning  the  rejection of  slow-varying disturbances, we
introduce a general EABC scheme that effectively handles
both matched and mismatched disturbances while ensuring

8
clined plane under four controllers. For clarity of illustration, all signals are
sing the filtfilt  function of MATLAB.  (b) Snapshots of the experiments.  (c)
g error during experiments with four controllers. The best values are in bold
zero steady-state tracking error. The track stand experiments
on an inclined plane validate the capability of EABC-MPC
to reject varying external disturbances, while the rear position
tracking experiments demonstrate the accurate tracking of a
varying reference. The experimental results demonstrate that
our method significantly outperforms ECBC-MPC,  IMPC,
and simple MPC in terms of  tracking errors by orders of
magnitude. In the future, we plan to apply the EABC scheme
to other stunt control  tasks,  such as wheelie,  stoppie,  and
drifting, to further enhance the practicality of STTW robots.
APPENDIX
A. Modeling of the STTW Robot

According to Fig. 3b, consider  the roll motion around the
